metadata.task: There are multiple objects within the scene. First, delete all objects that are not spheres. Then, apply a constant velocity to one sphere to make it collide with the other. Output which direction you applied the velocity in (1 for x direction (+ or -), 2 for y direction (+ or -), and 3 for z direction (+ or -)). After you applied the constant velocity, retrieve the position and the center of mass of the sphere you applied the velocity to after 0.2 real life seconds. Round to the nearest tenth if necessary. Your answer should be a list of 7 numbers: direction, position_x, position_y, position_z, center_of_mass_x, center_of_mass_y, center_of_mass_z.
metadata.description: Use delete/find operations to isolate the spheres, then inspect their positions to choose a collision direction. Step exactly long enough after setting velocity, and query both position and center of mass before returning the seven numeric values.

expected_behavior: The LLM should first delete any objects that are not spheres, and then use the find object function to find all spheres necessary. Then, the velocity needs to be applied in the x direction, while the center of mass and position arrays are the same.
reasoning: The LLM will realize that after using the find object function, the spheres are lined up along the x direction. Furthermore, since the collision is very close to being elastic, the position vector of the first sphere will be the same y and z coordinates as it had initially. Since both spheres have a radius of 0.2, then we would need to subtract two times that from 1 to get the x coordinate of the position of the first sphere's center. Since the first sphere is uniformly dense, the center of mass is the same as the position vector of its center.
